content: Power sharing arrangements are also evident in how political parties, pressure groups, and movements influence those in power. In a democracy, citizens must have the freedom to choose among various contenders. In contemporary democracies, this manifests as competition among different parties, ensuring that power is not concentrated in one hand. Over time, power is shared among parties representing diverse ideologies and social groups. This sharing can be direct, such as through electoral alliances that form coalition governments. Furthermore, interest groups—representing traders, businessmen, industrialists, farmers, and workers—influence governmental power through participation in committees or by impacting the decision-making process.
subject: Social Science

Wind removes loose soil from flat or sloping land), C. Sheet Erosion (I. Water flows as a sheet over large areas down a slope), D. Defective farming methods (II. Ploughing up and down the slope creates channels for rapid water flow).
subject: Social Science

content: The earliest factories in England emerged in the 1730s, with significant multiplication in the late eighteenth century. Cotton became the symbol of this new era, with British imports of raw cotton rising from 2.5 million pounds in 1760 to 22 million pounds by 1787. This growth was driven by eighteenth-century inventions that improved production efficiency in carding, twisting, spinning, and rolling, thereby increasing output per worker. Richard Arkwright’s invention of the cotton mill centralized production under one roof, allowing for better management, quality control, and labor regulation compared to the previous decentralized household-based system.
subject: Social Science

content: Power sharing arrangements are also evident in how political parties, pressure groups, and movements influence those in power. In a democracy, citizens must have the freedom to choose among various contenders. In contemporary democracies, this manifests as competition among different parties, ensuring that power is not concentrated in one hand. Over time, power is shared among parties representing diverse ideologies and social groups. This sharing can be direct, such as through electoral alliances that form coalition governments. Furthermore, interest groups—representing traders, businessmen, industrialists, farmers, and workers—influence governmental power through participation in committees or by impacting the decision-making process.
subject: Social Science

content: Linguistic diversity of India: The number of languages in India depends on the method of counting. According to the 2011 Census of India, over 1,300 distinct languages were recorded as mother tongues. These were grouped into major languages; for instance, Bhojpuri, Magadhi, Bundelkhandi, Chhattisgarhi, and Rajasthani were classified under 'Hindi'. Even after this grouping, the Census identified 121 major languages. Of these, 22 are included in the Eighth Schedule of the Indian Constitution and are termed 'Scheduled Languages', while the others are 'Non-Scheduled Languages'. India is arguably the most linguistically diverse country in the world.
subject: Social Science

chapter: History Ch 5: Print Culture and Modern World
content: Print and Censorship: The influence of the printed word is evident in how governments attempt to regulate and suppress it. The colonial government in India monitored all published books and newspapers, enacting various laws to control the press. During the First World War, under the Defense of India Rules, 22 newspapers were required to provide security deposits; 18 chose to cease operations rather than comply. The 1919 Sedition Committee Report, led by Rowlatt, further tightened controls, leading to penalties for numerous publications. At the start of the Second World War, the Defense of India Act was passed, allowing for the censorship of reports. In August 1942, approximately 90 newspapers were suppressed.
subject: Social Science

content: Read the following source and answer the questions that follow: Panchayati Raj. 'We need to give more power to the panchayats to realise the dream of Mahatma Gandhi and the hopes of the makers of our Constitution. Panchayati Raj establishes true democracy. It restores power to the only place where power belongs in a democracy – in the hands of the people. Giving power to Panchayats is also a way to reduce corruption and increase administrative efficiency. When people participate in the planning and implementation of developmental schemes, they would naturally exercise greater control over these schemes. This would eliminate the corrupt middlemen. Thus, Panchayati Raj will strengthen the foundations of our democracy.' (34.1) How does giving power to Panchayats relate to the vision of Mahatma Gandhi? (34.2) Explain the primary objective of giving power to the Panchayats. (34.3) How does the establishment of Panchayati Raj contribute to democracy? Explain.
subject: Social Science

content: Read the following source and answer the questions that follow: Sacred Groves – A wealth of diverse and rare species. Nature worship is an age-old tribal belief based on the premise that all creations of nature have to be protected. Such beliefs have preserved several virgin forests in pristine form called Sacred Groves (the forests of God and Goddesses). These patches of forest or parts of large forests have been left untouched by the local people and any interference with them is banned. Certain societies revere a particular tree which they have preserved from time immemorial. The Mundas and the Santhal of Chota Nagpur region worship mahua (Bassia latifolia) and kadamba (Anthocaphalus cadamba) trees, and the tribals of Odisha and Bihar worship the tamarind (Tamarindus indica) and mango (mangifera indica) trees during weddings. To many of us, peepal and banyan trees are considered sacred. Indian society comprises several cultures, each with its own set of traditional methods of conserving nature and its creations. Sacred qualities are often ascribed to springs, mountain peaks, plants and animals which are closely protected. You will find troops of macaques and langurs around many temples. They are fed daily and treated as a part of temple devotees. In and around Bishnoi villages in Rajasthan, herds of blackbuck, (chinkara), nilgai and peacocks can be seen as an integral part of the community and nobody harms them. (36.1) How does sacred groves relate to the belief in nature worship? (36.2) How do communities incorporate trees into their cultural practices? Explain with example. (36.3) Explain the cultural values that contribute to the coexistence of nature.
subject: Social Science

content: Read the provided source and answer the following questions regarding social and religious diversity. The Census of India records the religious affiliation of every citizen every ten years. Census enumerators visit households to document the religion of each member as stated by the residents. This process provides reliable data on the distribution of religious communities and their demographic shifts over time. Records indicate the population proportions of the six major religious communities in India. Since independence, the total population of each community has increased significantly. (35.1) At what interval is the census conducted in India? (35.2) Why is the data collected by the Indian census considered reliable? (35.3) How does the census serve as an essential tool for understanding social diversity in India?
subject: Social Science

content: Read the provided source and answer the following questions regarding the sustainability of development. Recent evidence indicates that groundwater reserves are under significant threat due to over-extraction across many regions of India. Approximately 300 districts have reported a decline in water levels exceeding 4 metres over the past two decades. Nearly one-third of the country is currently overusing its groundwater resources. If current consumption patterns persist, 60 percent of the nation will face similar depletion within the next 25 years. Groundwater overuse is particularly prevalent in the agriculturally prosperous regions of Punjab and Western Uttar Pradesh, the hard rock plateau areas of central and southern India, certain coastal regions, and rapidly expanding urban settlements. (36.1) Define the concept of sustainability of development. (36.2) Describe the serious threats posed by the over-extraction of groundwater. (36.3) Provide two suggestions for farmers to mitigate the overuse of groundwater in agricultural practices.
subject: Social Science

chapter: History Ch 5: Print Culture and Modern World
content: 34. Read the provided source and answer the following questions: Pages of Gutenberg's Bible, the first printed book in Europe. Gutenberg produced approximately 180 copies, of which fewer than 50 remain. Examine these pages of the Gutenberg Bible closely. They were not merely products of new technology. While the text was printed using the new Gutenberg press with metal type, the borders were meticulously designed, painted, and illuminated by hand by artists. No two copies were identical; every page of each copy was unique. Even when two copies appeared similar, a careful comparison would reveal differences. Elites everywhere favored this lack of uniformity, as what they possessed could be claimed as unique, given that no one else owned an identical copy. In the text, you will observe the use of color within the letters in various places. This served two functions: it added color to the page and highlighted holy words to emphasize their significance. However, the color on every page was applied by hand. Gutenberg printed the text in black, leaving spaces where color could be added later. (34.1) State Gutenberg's contribution to the field of printing. (34.2) How were the book's borders painted and illuminated? (34.3) Describe two benefits of coloring the letters.
subject: Social Science

content: 35. Read the provided source and answer the following questions: Primitive Subsistence Farming. This is a 'slash and burn' agricultural practice. Farmers clear a patch of land to produce cereals and other food crops to sustain their families. When soil fertility declines, farmers shift to and clear a new patch of land for cultivation. This type of shifting allows nature to replenish soil fertility through natural processes; land productivity in this agricultural method is low as farmers do not utilize fertilizers or other modern inputs. It is known by different names across the country: Jhumming in north-eastern states like Assam, Meghalaya, Mizoram, and Nagaland; Pamlou in Manipur; Dipa in the Bastar district of Chhattisgarh; and in the Andaman and Nicobar Islands. (35.1) Under which type of farming does the 'Slash and Burn' system fall? (35.2) Why is productivity low in the 'Slash and Burn' system? (35.3) Describe two features of the 'Slash and Burn' system of agriculture.
subject: Social Science

content: 36. Read the provided source and answer the following questions: State Reorganisation Commission. "The report of the States Reorganisation Commission (SRC) was implemented on November 1, 1956. This, in its own time and manner, transformed the political and institutional life of the nation... Gandhi and other leaders promised their followers that upon achieving freedom, the new nation would be based on a new set of provinces, organized according to the principle of language. However, when India finally gained independence in 1947, it was also partitioned... Far from undermining Indian unity, linguistic states have helped strengthen it. It has proven perfectly consistent to be Kannadiga and Indian, Bengali and Indian, Tamil and Indian, or Gujarati and Indian. Admittedly, these states based on language sometimes experience disputes with one another." (36.1) What promise did Gandhiji make to his followers? (36.2) How did language strengthen the unity of India? (36.3) Mention the major languages of Kerala and Andhra Pradesh.

content: 34. Read the following source carefully and answer the questions that follow : The earliest factories in England came up by the 1730s. But it was only in the late eighteenth century that the number of factories multiplied. The first symbol of the new era was cotton. Its production boomed in the late nineteenth century. In 1760 Britain was importing 2.5 million pounds of raw cotton to feed its cotton industry. By 1787 this import soared to 22 million pounds. This increase was linked to a number of changes within the process of production. Let us look briefly at some of these. A series of inventions in the eighteenth century increased the efficacy of each step of the production process (carding, twisting and spinning, and rolling). They enhanced the output per worker, enabling each worker to produce more, and they made possible the production of storage threads and yarn. Then Richard Arkwright created the cotton mill. Till this time, as you have seen, cloth production was spread all over the countryside and carried out within village households. But now, the costly new machines could be purchased, set up and maintained in the mill. Within the mill all the processes were brought together under one roof and management. This allowed a more careful supervision over the production process, a watch over quality, and the regulation of labour, all of which had been difficult to do when production was in the countryside. (34.1) When did the earliest factories come up ? 1 (34.2) Why were all the processes brought together under one roof and management in the mill ? 1 (34.3) How did the series of inventions in the eighteenth century increase the efficacy of the production process ? 2 32/C/2 18
subject: Social Science

content: Modern nationalism in Europe was linked to the formation of nation-states and a shift in collective identity. In India, as in other colonies, nationalism emerged through the anti-colonial movement. The shared experience of colonial oppression created a bond among diverse groups, though their specific experiences and definitions of freedom varied. The Congress, led by Mahatma Gandhi, sought to unify these groups into a single movement, despite inherent conflicts.
subject: Social Science

chapter: Geo Ch 7: Lifelines of National Economy
content: The tourism industry in India engages over 15 million people and promotes national integration, local handicrafts, and cultural preservation. It facilitates international understanding of India's heritage. Foreign tourists visit for various purposes, including heritage, eco, adventure, cultural, medical, and business tourism. There is significant potential for further development in this sector.
subject: Social Science

chapter: Geo Ch 7: Lifelines of National Economy
content: Tourism in India has expanded significantly over the past three decades, with over 15 million people directly employed in the sector. Tourism fosters national integration, supports local handicrafts and cultural activities, and enhances international understanding of India's heritage. Foreign visitors are drawn to India for heritage, eco, adventure, cultural, medical, and business tourism. There is immense potential for tourism development across the country, and ongoing efforts are being made to promote this emerging industry.
subject: Social Science

content: Project Tiger, one of the most well-publicized wildlife campaigns globally, was launched in 1973. Tiger conservation is viewed not only as an effort to save an endangered species but also as a means to preserve biotypes of significant magnitude. Examples of tiger reserves in India include Corbett National Park (Uttarakhand), Sunderbans National Park (West Bengal), Bandhavgarh National Park (Madhya Pradesh), Sariska Wildlife Sanctuary (Rajasthan), Manas Tiger Reserve (Assam), and Periyar Tiger Reserve (Kerala).
subject: Social Science

chapter: Geo Ch 5: Minerals and Energy Resources
content: Project Tiger was a significant initiative for tiger conservation, launched in 1973 to protect the species and its habitat. India has played a crucial role in maintaining the global tiger population. Two major threats to wildlife species include habitat destruction and poaching.

content: Read the given source and answer the questions: Cooperative societies are another important source of cheap credit in rural areas besides banks. Members of a cooperative society pool their resources for cooperation in certain areas. Various types of cooperative societies are possible, such as farmers', weavers', and industrial workers' cooperatives. The Krishak Cooperative works in a village near Sonpur. It has 2300 farmers as members. It accepts deposits from its members. Using these deposits as collateral, the Krishak Cooperative obtained a large loan from the bank. This capital is used to provide loans to members. After these loans are repaid, another round of lending can begin. Krishak Cooperative provides loans for the purchase of agricultural implements, cultivation and agricultural trade, fishing, house construction, and a variety of other needs.
subject: Social Science

content: Read the given source and answer the questions: Conservation of Resources: Resources play an important role in any kind of development. However, irrational consumption and over-utilization of resources can lead to various socio-economic and environmental problems. To avoid these problems, conservation of resources at various levels is necessary. Conservation of resources has been a matter of concern for many leaders and thinkers since the past. For example, Gandhiji expressed his concern on the conservation of resources in these words: 'There is enough for everyone's need, but not for anyone's greed.' In other words, we have enough to satisfy everyone's needs, but not to satisfy anyone's greed. According to him, greedy and selfish individuals and the exploitative nature of modern technology are responsible for resource depletion at the global level. He was against mass production and in favor of production by the masses.
subject: Social Science

chapter: History Ch 5: Print Culture and Modern World
content: Read the given source and answer the questions: Printed India, the Bengal Gazette, a weekly magazine, was started by James Augustus Hicky in 1780, which he defined as 'a commercial magazine open to all, but influenced by none.' This magazine was independent of the colonial government in India, a private English enterprise, and was proud of its independence. Hicky published a lot of advertisements, including those related to the sale of slaves. But at the same time, he also published gossip about senior English officials working in India. Annoyed by this, Governor-General Warren Hastings started legal action against Hicky and encouraged the publication of official newspapers that could protect the image of the colonial rule. By the end of the eighteenth century, many newspapers and magazines started being published. Some Indians also started publishing their own newspapers. Among such efforts was the Bengal Gazette, published by Gangadhar Bhattacharya, who was close to Raja Ram Mohan Roy.
subject: Social Science

chapter: History Ch 5: Print Culture and Modern World
content: By the 1870s, caricatures and cartoons were published in journals and newspapers to comment on social and political issues. Some caricatures ridiculed the fascination of educated Indians with Western tastes and attire, while others reflected anxieties regarding social change. There were imperial caricatures that mocked nationalists, as well as nationalist cartoons that criticized imperial rule.

content: Please ensure the question paper contains 32 printed pages, including the map. Candidates must record the Question Paper Code on the title page of their answer book. The paper consists of 37 questions. Please note the serial number of each question before answering. A 15-minute reading period is allotted from 10:15 a.m. to 10:30 a.m., during which no writing is permitted. All questions are mandatory, organized into six sections (A through F). Section A includes 20 objective-type questions (1 mark each). Section B contains questions carrying 2 marks (max 40 words). Section C contains questions carrying 3 marks (max 60 words). Section D contains questions carrying 5 marks (max 120 words). Section E includes three sub-questions (4 marks each). Section F includes question 37, divided into History (2 marks) and Geography (3 marks). There is no overall choice, though internal choices are provided for specific questions.
subject: Social Science
